original text:
"I do not like carving the world into segments; we are one world", said the only lady Prime Minister of India, Indira Gandhi. Daughter of the nation's very first Prime Minister, Jawaharlal Nehru, Indira Gandhi was one of the longest serving Prime Minister of the country with a reign of around 15 years.
Known for her remarkable speeches and strong views on various social and political issues, Indira Gandhi often took the crowd by storm with her extraordinary presence. Outspoken, yet humble, she had much to contribute to the nation.
On her 34th death anniversary, to pay tribute to this powerful leader, we bring to you a collection of some inspirational sayings by Indira Gandhi on knowledge, freedom, power, and feminism.
* Happiness is a state of mind, you know. I don't think you are permanently happy. One is happy about certain things and not so happy about others.
* To be liberated, a woman must feel free to be herself, not in rivalry to man but in the context of her own capacity and her personality.
* Popularity is not a guarantee of quality.
* The greatest of all contraceptives is affluence.
* You soon realize that the peak you've climbed was one of the lowest, that the mountain was part of a chain of mountains, that there are still so many, so many mountains to climb...And the more you climb, the more you want to climb - even though you're dead tired.
* The purpose of life is to believe, to hope, and to strive.
* You must learn to be still in the midst of activity and vibrantly alive in repose.
* Difficulties can't be eliminated from life. Individuals will always have them, countries will always have them...The only thing is to accept them, if possible overcome them, otherwise to come to terms with them. It's all right to fight, yes, but only when it's possible".
* Whenever you take a step forward, you are bound to disturb something.

* Whenever you take a step forward, you are bound to disturb something.

original text:
Does a Baroque bow need to be more tensioned than a modern bow?
Not really sure about boroque bow tension, but I would think you would need enough to make it playable like a modern bow and be able to adjust the tension with the screw, (if it has one), I'm unable to find the pic you posted of it.
As far as hair width, a hank of bow hair usuall contain's 150-200 hair's. If the hair is narrower at the frog, there is a possibility that the wooden shim/spreader has come out between the hair and the ferrule. This happened with my pernambuco bow I had rehaired last year. I noticed that the hair was very narrow at the frog, but normal width at the tip, I knew I hadn't lost any hair's, so when I took it to another luthier, he said it hadn't been rehaired properly and that the wood shim/spreader had fallen out letting the hair's clump together, they were only about 7 mm wide. After it was fixed, the hair's now measure 11mm at the frog.
Incidentally, you really don't realize how fine the hair's are untill you start counting them. One of my bow's with "Fiddlershop" hair counted 178 hair's. Definately not good on the eye's.
No, it's the same thickness all the way. It looks as if it's as it should be.
And yes, it does have a screw.
Its OK to use but I find that it handles better with a higher tension.
I can't find a great deal about them on the web except for their history.
They have quite an odd bow. They are also tuned in fifths like a violin. They look like a violin that has been in a bad car accident and when reassembled, they didn't have all the parts.
One great thing about the Erhu, is that the bow never get's seperated or lost from the instrument itself.
I hope you have a wonderfull time in China, I have a friend in, Taiyuan, city, Shanxi Province.
Be safe, John and good.
Not sure about the required tension either. The whole thing about bow tension in general is to find the tension that is not so loose that the bow stick touches and scrapes the strings too much when playing loudly and not so tight that the bow bounces uncontrollably.
In other words, you'll need to guess at first and experiment and find the best compromise.
hmm...since the cambering in the bow is the same as a modern bow, it should be the same as the tension of the modern bow, as long as it is not touching the stick and it's easy to do techniques, but it has a snakewood stick, so it's harder than the more flexible pernambuco........ erhus are awesome!!!!

yes, John, did not have all the parts so they substitute with something else handy beside the fact that somebody stole 2 strings and leaving only two strings left. Your future Erhu may have its "top plate" made with snake skin, python skin, perhaps, lol. Well, there were sheep guts and horse hairs for fiddles, and snake skin is also organic, right?
Have a safe trip and enjoy your time there, John.

original text:
We accept all Major Credit Cards, Debit Cards and PayPal.
We recommended using PayPal due to its simplicity, speed, security, and protection for both buyers and sellers.
In order to securely safeguard your personal information we use Trustwave's 256-bit SSL certification on Designer Man, along with live security protocols which protect your details when using the website. For more information please do not hesitate to contact us.
Service: UK Next Day Delivery (Fully tracked and Insured).
Delivery Time: Next working day.
Service: International Signed For (Fully Tracked and Insured).
Click Here to View Full Delivery Information Page.
The Designer Man returns process is simple, please simply fill out our returns form and one of our team will assist you in the returns process.
We require you to return the item(s) using a securely tracked and insured postal method as we cannot be liable for lost returns. Unfortunately due to ever-increasing costs we are unable to offer free returns at present at Designer Man, therefore you must cover the return costs to us.
For exchanges, the return postage to us is paid for by you, and the exchanged product may be due a standard, flat rate P&P charge. Please contact for further information.
Upon receiving the returned good(s), we will process and complete the refund, which usually takes around 2-5 working days, dependent on bank. Refund consists of initial order value, minus initial original postage and packaging charges.
If you have any further questions regarding our returns or exchange process please contact us.

original text:
I'm trying to rig a camera inside a car to get something like these shots from Drive.
I would want the camera somewhere around the passenger seat with the rearview mirror angled to see the driver and someone in the back seat.
We will be shooting on a Red Epic.
Thanks for any ideas or examples on how to rig a set up like this.
You can take off the passenger seat and use a trihat or something like that.
Baby legs centered in the back seat with 2 legs down on the floor and 1 leg up on the seat. Fluid head and possibly a Tango Head for level.
Handheld with a furni pad for support.
Remove the back seat and use a bazooka base and risers.
Speedrail hoodmount across rear seat and ratcheted to rear doors.
The best way would be to remove the passenger seat and try to rig a tripod there. If that's impossible, try to recline the passenger seat and see if there's anyway to rig something on top.
I'm afraid that any handheld shooting into the little rearview mirror will look way too shaky when the car is moving. But, perhaps you'll end up loving that effect? It's worth a try, but don't do it hand held unless you can deactivate the airbag. It could well be deadly if the airbag deploys into a camera and operator.

original text:
Heron Island is a coral cay on the Southern Great Barrier Reef. It has a modern resort and Heron Island Dive Shop has everything you need to enjoy diving Heron Island.
Heron Island is completely surrounded by coral reef which is accessible by reef walking, scuba diving and snorkelling. Heron Island Resort has 109 rooms, a bar, restaurant and all the facilities you would expect of a modern resort such as a pool and meeting centre.
All in water activities are run from the Marine Centre, and these include diving and a semisubmersible reef viewing boat. There are a number of nature based activities such as reef walking and bird watching arranged via the Information Centre.
The island's nature calendar includes the nesting and hatching of turtles, the nesting and hatching of sea birds and hump-back whale migration.
From a diving perspective it's paradise: three dives per day, plus night dives, in reasonably shallow clear water, with most dive sites just minutes from the jetty.
Heron Island is located 82 km north-east of Gladstone on a coral cay.
The Marine Centre is the Heron Island ‘dive shop'. It's the place where you book in for all watersports (including the semi-submersible trip), and hire any gear you need. Get all the detail via the link below.
There are dive sites all around the reef that surrounds Heron Island, and the adjacent Wistari Reef, so there is always a suitable spot whichever way the wind is blowing. The sites are imaginatively named after the features of the reef near the moorings that mark them and include lairs, cascades, bommies, holes, pools, grottos, gullies, canyons, points, peaks and gardens! The signature dive is Heron Bommie which is due west of the island and almost the closest dive site. In addition to the Heron Bommie dive, the other two dives below are both on the northern edge of the reef.
From a diving perspective, I would recommend getting a diving package and seeing what accommodation options are available with it. All rooms have a balcony or terrace - essential for drying your gear, and quite honestly, do you really need a view of the reef from your window - you're going to be out on it most of the time.
Still, there may be others in your group who want a little luxury, or direct access to the beach if that's where they'll be spending their day, while you're out on the boat.
Bottom lines: there are plenty to choose from; it's all good; you can even check into your room at the Marina in Gladstone before you get on the ferry.
There are specific 3, 5 and 7 day packages advertised for divers so check out these first.
Check availability with the Heron Island Resort directly.
The Shearwater Restaurant is the eatery - though some food is served at Baillies Bar. Breakfast is served as a buffet, lunches are a la carte, and dinner is also a la carte apart from the Friday and Saturday evening themed buffets.
The resort will allocate you are dining slot on the à la carte evenings so that there is not the long wait that there would be if everybody decided that 7pm was their dinnertime. You can book a specific slot in advance though if you choose.
The accommodation packages can be taken as inclusive of breakfast or, all meals. The restaurant has an extensive Australian beer and wine list and as it is a resort you are not permitted to take your own alcohol (or indeed food - there are no self-catering options).
Bailies bar has live music most evenings, two pool tables, and an excellent choice of beers on tap at reasonable prices.
The pool is right next the bar so you can safely watch the kids enjoy a drink and the fantastic sunsets.
About an hour before which the bar terrace which overlooks the lagoon has the most beautiful light - perfect for photography.
Check the Heron Times - published daily. Activities are pretty much the same each day, so don't worry if you don't have today's edition. The following daily activities are complimentary.
Research Walk: this is a guided tour through the University of Queensland's Research station. The world-class research and teaching facility is the largest island research centre in the Southern Hemisphere.
Snorkel lesson: join a 15 minute snorkel lesson (- though as a diver you should be giving the lessons if any of your mob need them).
Bird walk: learn about the many birds that are encountered on the island, including the residents, those that come just to breed like the terns and shearwaters, some migratory waders that come to escape the miserable northern winters, and local visitors from the mainland.
Island Walk: there are walking tracks which wind through the bushy centre of the island to each of the different beaches and reefs.
Depending on the weather, there may also be stargazing at the helipad after-dinner; a nature-based talk on some specific aspect of Heron's ecosystem or a sunset cruise.
The Information Centre: don't forget to check out the information centre as soon as you can. You'll learn a lot of useful background information about the island and the reef ecosystem.
The following activities attract a fee.
Semisubmersible: this is not your average glass bottomed boat trip. The boat in which you sit has a fully submersed lower section (which is covered overhead) so that you can see through the glass side panels and have a very clear and undistorted view of the reef and the amazing fish life to either side. Bookings via the Marine Centre.
Kayak hire: hired by the hour, near the jetty, you can grab a kayak to paddle around the lagoon during the afternoon hours.
Junior Rangers: ditch the kids and go and have a dive (or a rest). The Junior Rangers program was developed by Heron's team of marine biologists and naturalists with some help from the University of Queensland and is designed for kids aged between 7 and 12 years old. For $15 per child, per session, the kids get a Junior Rangers workbook, hat and get to earn embroidered badges by completing various tasks. For younger kids, there is safe paddling or swimming in the shallow lagoon or swimming pool.
Whale watching (in season): The whales that pass by Heron are Hump-backed whales - named after their small, but pronounced, dorsal fin. Reaching up to 16m in length and weighing up to 50 tonnes, they can be seen breaching from the jetty and shore line. June is the best time to watch for whales heading north to their tropical breeding grounds; September is the best time to see them heading back down south to their feeding grounds around Antarctica.
The Aqua Soul Spa: if you feel the need for a little pampering, book in for a facial, a Pedi or the works at Reception or at the Spa directly. If you're not sure what to choose, join the daily 15 minute tour from 4pm daily.
Turtles: Turtle nesting takes place between November and March. Turtles can be found simply by walking along Heron Beach one or two hours after the evening's high tide. Look for the tracks in the sand where the turtle has come in from the sea.
Hatchlings appear two months later (between January and May), again usually after dark. When the hatchlings emerge from the sand they use light horizons to navigate out to sea. If bright artificial light is present, they will get confused and may head toward it instead of the ocean.
In order to not disrupt their habitat, nesting habits, or the behaviour of hatchlings strict guidelines are in place for the viewing of nesting and hatching turtles. Please ensure you are aware of these guidelines before you head off to observe this natural phenomenon.
Of course, you can make up your own activities or just enjoy the island and beach on your own.
It's the divers' dilemma that the clearest water is always the coldest. Here are the average temperatures in centigrade.
There are two options for getting to Heron Island from Gladstone: the Heron Islander boat and by air. (Both Virgin Australia and Qantaslink fly to Gladstone).
The Heron Islander is a large (34m) comfortable boat with a bar, and usually a movie for the kids. It has two deck levels, and inside (air-conditioned) and outside seating. The boat travels to Heron Island and back 11 days out of every fortnight. Departing the Gladstone Marina at 11am arriving Heron at 1pm, and departing Heron at 2pm arriving back at Gladstone around 4pm.
There is a coach connection from the airport departing 10.15 am directly to the Marina; and another that transfers from the arriving boat back to the airport arriving just before 5pm. You will need to ensure your flights fit within this schedule, allowing a little contingency time for possible delays.
The alternatives to the Heron Islander are the seaplanes operated by Australia by Seaplane, or the helicopter.
There is no airstrip at Heron, so in-coming flights land on the water in the lagoon adjacent the jetty and depart as soon as they have deposited or collected passengers.
This means that flights are weather and tide dependent, as they need enough water to land and take off again on. It also means that you will need to wade ashore at Heron Island so wear shorts and sandals for the trip. It's a very scenic 25-35 minute trip though, so you should have your camera handy throughout.
There are two planes - one which departs from Bundaberg Airport, and one which takes off from Awoonga Lake which is a 30 min drive from Bundaberg airport, the transfers being included in the price. The planes only take three passengers, so it is pretty much your own private charter. The standard luggage allowance is 15 kg per person.
The helicopter transfers are operated by Heli Reef, and dependent on availability, can be arranged to work around your other travel requirements. The baggage allowance is 15 kg per person, but additional luggage can be transferred via the launch the day prior at no additional charge.
You can mix and match your arrival and departure methods, though it you are going to do this, you will need to probably need to speak to a person rather than attempt to book on-line.
Though you can charge everything to your room for your stay, take some cash to cover your last lunch after you've checked out.

There is a coach connection from the airport departing 10.15 am directly to the Marina; and another that transfers from the arriving boat back to the airport arriving just before 5pm. You will need to ensure your flights fit within this schedule, allowing a little contingency time for possible delays.
The alternatives to the Heron Islander are the seaplanes operated by Australia by Seaplane, or the helicopter.
There is no airstrip at Heron, so in-coming flights land on the water in the lagoon adjacent the jetty and depart as soon as they have deposited or collected passengers.
This means that flights are weather and tide dependent, as they need enough water to land and take off again on. It also means that you will need to wade ashore at Heron Island so wear shorts and sandals for the trip. It's a very scenic 25-35 minute trip though, so you should have your camera handy throughout.
There are two planes - one which departs from Bundaberg Airport, and one which takes off from Awoonga Lake which is a 30 min drive from Bundaberg airport, the transfers being included in the price. The planes only take three passengers, so it is pretty much your own private charter. The standard luggage allowance is 15 kg per person.
The helicopter transfers are operated by Heli Reef, and dependent on availability, can be arranged to work around your other travel requirements. The baggage allowance is 15 kg per person, but additional luggage can be transferred via the launch the day prior at no additional charge.
You can mix and match your arrival and departure methods, though it you are going to do this, you will need to probably need to speak to a person rather than attempt to book on-line.
Though you can charge everything to your room for your stay, take some cash to cover your last lunch after you've checked out.

original text:
Well For Culture co-founder addressed the crowd with a powerful speech at the 2019 Phoenix Womxn's March.
What does your baby really need? Probably a lot less than you think. In this blog, Chelsey offers her tips on purchasing and sourcing baby items, avoiding the corporate hype and figuring out what you should actually spend money on. The minimalist approach will help to keep you stress-free and decluttered.
We get this question every day: why don't we show our baby's face on social media? Read to find out.
Read this for tips on maintaining optimal wellness while traveling by air or land with your little one in tow.
Staying active on the road can be a challenge due to lack of time or even motivation. One piece of equipment that will help maximize your gains in a short period and may even motivate you to be active with the little time you have is a resistance band.
Foods, movement, and a healthy routine can play a major role in substance abuse recovery. To find out more, read this.

original text:
Among the more than 2 million registered nurses across the country, only about 10 percent are men, according to the latest census data. For Chris Stallard, MSN, RN, FNP-C, COHC, that statistic is troubling. He wants to know what is holding men back from pursuing a career in nursing. He believes that promoting more diversity and inclusion in the nursing community leads to better care for patients.
Stallard is a family nurse practitioner with Professional Performance Development Group, Inc. and an occupational health nurse for Johnson & Johnson in Texas and Arkansas. Stallard first wanted to be a nurse after helping provide relief during both Hurricane Katrina and Hurricane Rita. While helping, he realized that he enjoyed how nursing let him connect with people, and he immediately told his wife that he wanted to be a nurse. The couple moved to San Angelo, Tex., and Stallard started nursing school.
While in nursing school, Stallard noticed that students often learned about female nursing pioneers like Florence Nightingale and Dorothea Dix, but heard very little about the rich history of men in nursing, dating back to the first nursing school (for men) created in India in 250 B.C. or Friar Juan de Mena, the first identified nurse in what was to become the U.S., 70 years before the Pilgrims landed on Plymouth Rock.
According to Stallard, a slight bias against men in nursing often begins in the classroom and continues into many aspects of nursing practice. Even the term "male nurse" is in itself a barrier.
However, for many patients, the term "male nurse" is insignificant.
Stallard hopes other men will consider nursing as a career.
And for female nurses, interested in addressing the issues he describes for men in nursing?
For more information about the history of men in nursing, visit DiscoverNursing.com. You can also see a short video featuring Tom, a pediatric oncology nurse, below. Additionally, nurses interested in connecting with a community of men in nursing can join the American Assembly for Men in Nursing (AAMN). The AAMN works to support men who are nurses and demonstrate to society the increasing contributions being made by men within the nursing profession.

ANNOUNCEMENT :: Rime Arodaky gowns are making their way from Paris to Minneapolis. Beauties, this is your opportunity to see our newest designer's full collection!
Rime Arodaky creates a new wave of bridal gowns, breaking away from the traditional wide skirts and corseted bustiers. With a nod to the 70's and the rock'n roll bride, Rime has unleashed the inner bridal goddess in her designs with sensual details and romantic, yet innovative cuts.
Call 612.361.7175 // e-mail  for details! This week will book fast, beauties. Be sure to secure your appointment today!
Our favorite Americana designer is sending back all of her brand new creations to our showroom and we cannot wait!
Chantel Lauren is for the wild at heart and the romantic spirit. Her gowns are organic and artistic, featuring hand painted skirts in customizable colors. From fringe detailing to bell sleeves, these gowns will bring out your inner country rocker!
All of her pieces are 1 0 % O F F all weekend long, beauties.
The White Room is so excited to feature Lola Varma for the first time ever!
This fashion forward Australian brand is for the tailored fashionista who feels most confident in slinky silk or sophisticated separates.
The Lola Varma bride is looking for an  E F F OR T L E S S  elegance on her wedding day.
We are offering 1 0 % O F F these beauties all weekend long!
She stole our hearts with her upbeat attitude and her audacious designs, and now we cannot wait to have her back in our showroom!
Sarah prides herself on a body positive brand and believes that a bride deserves a gown tailored just for her on her big day // that's why each gown is bespoke to its bride's individual measurements. Also- her gowns are just plain FUN.
All gowns are handmade in Chicago by talented people paid a living wage // can we get a HELL YEAH to that?!
Bridal beauties // Sarah Seven is back at it with her AMAZING new 2019 collection // these gowns are the perfect marriage of traditional bride and modern muse // her daring necklines combined with graceful details are young, fresh, and leave you feeling like a totally sophisticated babe.
The L A W bridal was created to provide a range of gowns that cater to women with a clear and individual sense of style.
The White Room is excited to announce that we will be featuring their N E W collection, full of minimalist silhouettes with exceptional details that are modern, polished, and cool.
F R O M  T H E  D E S I G N E R S // " We encourage our brides to wear The LAW, and then write their own."
// We will be offering  1 0 % O F F  any THE LAW dress during this wonderful event // AND, the designer herself will be here to help you select the dress of your dreams.
Exciting news, beauties! New designer to the bridal scene, THENEWHITE, will be debuting her newest collection this weekend!
Come meet the designer, Juliette, sip on some bubbly & see these new gowns in our beautiful showroom!
The fun-loving local ladies of linyage are at it again with a brand new collection!
"... linyage is a design house focused on creating one-of-a-kind, vintage inspired bridal dresses, lingerie, lace toppers and beyond. linyage dresses are born out of a love for hand-crafted work, an appreciation for charming details, and a fondness for the character inherent to vintage lace. Like the name suggests, linyage is about drawing a connection between roots and growth...leaving you with a full heart and an heirloom to leave future lineage."
ALON LIVNE has landed in MPLS and we are so excited to announce that we will be showing his N E W E S T bridal collection for a limited time // if you are on the hunt for some serious jawdroppers, LOOK NO FURTHER.
DAN JONES LABEL is an effortless fusion of luxe fabrics, shape defining silhouettes and exquisite craftsmanship.
 You do NOT want to miss this weekend.
We are excited to present the N E W 2019 collection of Atelier Pronovias. Designed in Barcelona, these beauties are wonderfully elegant yet innovative designs. Made from luxurious fabrics, these wedding gowns are bound to leave your guests (and future hubs) breathless.
The ladies from Brooklyn are back at it with their edgy, yet sophisticated designs. The White Room will be presenting their 2018 collection and, ladies, these looks are to D I E for.
Come meet the designers, sip on bubbly, and find your dream gown!
Beauties! If you are looking for that romantic, ethereal & free-spirited gown, Stephanie's designs are the perfect dream dress for you.
Odylyne The Ceremony is a line of exquisite bridal gowns designed by Stephanie White- each dress embodies the deep emotion, sensibility and beauty of the life and art. Her collections tie in the feelings of the traditional foundations of marriage to the exotic and unfamiliar, while harnessing the power of the imagination to envision and to escape. The ethereal element is ever present in each style with a twist of romance and deep appreciation of all the delicate moments of the ceremony.
10% off these gorgeous designs all weekend long.
Be sure to make your appointment before the weekend books up!
One-of-a-kind brides // this is THE weekend to find your dream dress.
We are obsessed with Katherine Polk's designs, and are so excited to show our Minnesota brides the chic, emerging bridal trends coming from NYC.
10% off any Houghton gown this weekend- beauties, you do not want to miss this presentation!
Brides-to-be, if you are looking for romantic cuts and beautiful laces in your wedding gown, you absolutely MUST stop by to see the gorgeous Claire Pettibone Romantique collection! Made with love in LA, these beauties feature delicate lace, elegant silhouettes and the designer's signature stunning backs.
This collection is only around for the weekend, darlings.

Locally-based Caroline Hayden is at it again with a beautiful presentation of her current collection from F/W '16 and S/S '17.
Caroline Hayden has been featured in numbers publications and websites, including Harper's Bazaar.com, Rose and Ivy Journal and Minnesota Bride! Her cutting edge designs are unique, using carefully chosen fabrics and embellishments.
Walk-ins welcome on Saturday, November 26th // main fitting room reserved by appointment.
Attention, beauties! This is your chance to see the stunning designs from renowned Australian designer, Mariana Hardwick for a limited time at The White Room!
"... inspired by the grand couturiers of the past, Mariana Hardwick's designs have a distinct contemporary orientation. Drawing inspiration from from contemporary couture, vintage references, texture and drape, the Mariana Hardwick gown is timeless."
Available for 10 days in the showroom // be sure to book your appointment today!

Forever evolving their vision and the pursuit of the perfect gown, Marquise Bride is constantly redefining bridal trends while continuing on with the fundamental theme of simple uncomplicated silhouettes using luxury textiles.
The result is sultry, romantic and luxury gowns for the modern bride.
This collection will be available exclusively to The White Room for a two-week long exhibition of affordable luxury.
Be sure to make your appointment today to see these beauties!
We are excited to announce a month long showcase with our local gal pal, Chelsea Lovett with Modehaus Designs! This talented lady has created sleek modern designs that cater to a bride looking for a classic statement.

The oh-so-talented local jewelry maker, Sara Goldfine of Goldfine Jewelry will be showing off some of her stunning pieces during the next month to accessorize your wedding look to perfection.
15% off these stunners all month long!
Gorgeous Spanish designs from Belén Barbero are coming to MN for one week only!
The White Room will be offering 10% off these gorgeous gowns to our brides for the weekend!
Be sure to book your appointment today.
Suzanne Harward Pop-Up Trunk Show!
Nothing looks more striking on a woman than confidence. Alyssa Kristin collections are thoughtfully designed so that each piece, while varied in style and Silhouette, features a distinctive element - a dramatic train, a unique neckline, a removable skirt. This allows a bride to accentuate her favorite feature, whether it is her back, collar bone or hour glass figure, and radiate confidence.
The White Room is excited to announce that Alyssa will have her beautiful wedding gowns available at The White Room for an exclusive weekend! Come look through these gorgeous gowns & meet-and-greet with designer Alyssa!
7175 for details or use the 'Book and Appointment' button on the right side of the screen!
Appointments are bound to fill up quickly, be sure to book yours today!
Timeless and elegant for the modern day bride, the Rosa Clara bridal gown collection is luxuriously chic and utilizes the world finest couture fabrications. A bride who wears Rosa Clara will feel unique and special in her beautifully tailored creation. This is the weekend for brides looking to make a timeless statement.
10% off all Rosa Clara gowns will be honored during this showcase.
7175 or click "Book and Appointment" on the right side of the screen to schedule you appointment today!
The White Room will showase a curated selection of Elizabeth Fillmore gowns during the weekend, showing off the beautiful vairety of gowns the designer has to offer!
10% off any Elizabeth Fillmore gown during this weekend.
7175 or e-mail us HELLO@THEWHITEROOMMPLS.COM for details!
We are so excited to announce our FIRST trunk show since opening our doors- Come see the beautiful, newly released 2016 Rosa Clara collection at The White Room!
We will be hosting private appointments on Friday, October 23rd in the evening & all-day Saturday, October 24th. An open house will be hosted during the day on Friday, October 23rd & all day on Sunday, October 25th for the brides looking to stop in and look through the collection.
Call/E-mail us for more information!

original text:
Sally and Greg Brown, in their late 50s, should be planning for retirement, dreaming of their future.
But no one knows what that future will hold.
A husband. A wife. Married for 33 years. So much love. So much history. But so much fear.
She is worried about him, and his cancer diagnosis. He is worried about her, with a more recent cancer diagnosis of her own.
Greg, 57, was diagnosed with multiple myeloma five years ago. Sally, who works as a nurse at Spectrum Health Zeeland Community Hospital, has been at his side, caring for him, both inside the hospital and at home.
My immediate emotion was how much more can we take? I think I was more devastated by her diagnosis than my own. I was doing better, beating all the odds ... then she got diagnosed.
While Greg underwent two stem cell transplants, Sally, who had been a nurse at Spectrum Health since 1993, went back to school to get her bachelor's degree. She planned for a future. Possibly without her husband.
Two years prior to his multiple myeloma diagnosis, Greg battled prostate cancer.
The Browns were full-up with the "C" word. Done. Enough.
In the spring of 2016, Sally felt a lump in her breast. After battling benign cysts much of her life, she didn't think much of it.
On June 7, 2016, the "C" word returned. Diagnosis? Stage 3 breast cancer.
There's disbelief, certainly. Confusion, for sure. Wishing things could be different? Definitely.
Because of family history, Sally had been faithful to regular breast MRIs.
In that 18 months, Stage 3 triple negative cancer took root in her body.
Colleen App, MD, a breast surgeon with the Spectrum Health Cancer Center, delivered the diagnosis.
It numbed her. Stunned her. Chilled her.
Her grandmother and mom both battled breast cancer. She, the heir apparent.
And a husband, facing a terminal diagnosis. But hope remains.
Because Greg's disease made it impossible for him to continue his work in the forklift industry, Sally has been the family's sole supporter. Not only financially. "I try to be as much as possible emotional support for him," she said.
She had to undergo her own cancer battle - six months of chemotherapy, which caused blisters and hives on her helping hands and 37 days of radiation.
In January of this year, she had a left modified radical mastectomy and 21 lymph nodes removed. In November, she's planning on complete breast reconstruction.
You start to look at what really matters, what really counts. I just look at every day you wake up as a good day.
"The chemo must have worked because there's no sign of disease at this point," Sally said.
She returned to work, fittingly, the day before Independence Day. Free, she hopes, from cancer. Forever.
Greg and Sally are living life the best they know how right now, traveling to the east coast to visit their daughters, traveling north to spend time at their cottage on Portage Lake near Manistee, Michigan.
They boat. They fish. They dream of the future.
She and Greg are ready for more pomp. And less circumstance.
Greg agrees. He remembers being diagnosed around the same time their daughter started medical school in Boston.
So make that more icing. Lots of icing.
They're living within reality, but transcending beyond it. For as long as possible.
Amy Vander Woude, MD, an oncologist at the Spectrum Health Cancer Center, said Sally's triple negative breast cancer carries a high risk of recurrence.
Dr. Vander Woude said because no residual tumor was detected after chemotherapy, Sally has a decreased risk of developing recurrent disease in the future.
Such a warrior! Such a inspiration! Much love!
rock on, Browns! Positive vibes and tremendous best wishes for both of you incredible survivors!
Keep strong and keep praying. Your story is heartbreaking yet gives hope for tomorrow. May God bless you both with a lasting cure. Thank you for sharing your story.
My husband and I are also both battling cancer, after 40 years of marriage. We know the courage it takes. May Greg and Sally continue to find peace and enjoy every day they have together.
Thank you for your kind words of encouragement, Linda. We also wish you and your husband the very best of each day.
Stay strong! My husband and I share a similar story....I am 5 years out and a survivor. He responded well to his multiple myeloma stem cell transplant 2 years ago! We enjoy the gift of every day!
Congratulations to you and your husband, Joan! Thank you for sharing your wisdom and encouragement!
Greg and Sally, you are both so strong in your love for one another and keeping close one day at a time. Keep your head held high and cherish all the special moments you are given. I am a 20 year breast cancer survivor and am thankful for every moment my husband and I share together. God bless you both!

She had to undergo her own cancer battle - six months of chemotherapy, which caused blisters and hives on her helping hands and 37 days of radiation.
In January of this year, she had a left modified radical mastectomy and 21 lymph nodes removed. In November, she's planning on complete breast reconstruction.
You start to look at what really matters, what really counts. I just look at every day you wake up as a good day.
Greg and Sally are living life the best they know how right now, traveling to the east coast to visit their daughters, traveling north to spend time at their cottage on Portage Lake near Manistee, Michigan.
They boat. They fish. They dream of the future.
She and Greg are ready for more pomp. And less circumstance.
Greg agrees. He remembers being diagnosed around the same time their daughter started medical school in Boston.
So make that more icing. Lots of icing.
They're living within reality, but transcending beyond it. For as long as possible.
Amy Vander Woude, MD, an oncologist at the Spectrum Health Cancer Center, said Sally's triple negative breast cancer carries a high risk of recurrence.
Dr. Vander Woude said because no residual tumor was detected after chemotherapy, Sally has a decreased risk of developing recurrent disease in the future.
Such a warrior! Such a inspiration! Much love!
rock on, Browns! Positive vibes and tremendous best wishes for both of you incredible survivors!
Keep strong and keep praying. Your story is heartbreaking yet gives hope for tomorrow. May God bless you both with a lasting cure. Thank you for sharing your story.
My husband and I are also both battling cancer, after 40 years of marriage. We know the courage it takes. May Greg and Sally continue to find peace and enjoy every day they have together.
Thank you for your kind words of encouragement, Linda. We also wish you and your husband the very best of each day.
Stay strong! My husband and I share a similar story....I am 5 years out and a survivor. He responded well to his multiple myeloma stem cell transplant 2 years ago! We enjoy the gift of every day!
Congratulations to you and your husband, Joan! Thank you for sharing your wisdom and encouragement!
Greg and Sally, you are both so strong in your love for one another and keeping close one day at a time. Keep your head held high and cherish all the special moments you are given. I am a 20 year breast cancer survivor and am thankful for every moment my husband and I share together. God bless you both!
